question_frequency.rationale: The Pair asks two meaningful didactic questions: which approach to choose (A vs B) and a follow-up probing why Option B subtracts multiples of 15. For a one-step problem this is reasonable, though no further questions emerged after the user's response.

question_quality.rationale: The choice between brute-force loop and closed-form arithmetic series hits the actual crux of the problem (inclusion-exclusion + O(1) vs O(N)). The follow-up question about why we subtract multiples of 15 directly targets the conceptual heart of the formula.

scaffolding.rationale: Good progression: present the fork, ask the conceptual 'why' question, then after the user picks A, point out the regime where B becomes mandatory (N=10^12) and suggest a small hand-check to verify equivalence. No spoilers, smooth gradient.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and the Pair gracefully accepts the user's pragmatic choice while flagging the scaling caveat. Slight friction from a visible 'tool isn't available' meta-note and duplicate option presentation, but the back-and-forth is otherwise natural.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code (loop & filter) prints 233168 correctly, matching the baseline answer. It's a working solution though less elegant than the baseline's pure-math derivation; comparable in correctness.

friction.rationale: Two turns / 47s vs baseline's 1 turn / 14s — roughly 3x longer, but the extra time bought a real teaching moment (inclusion-exclusion explanation, scaling caveat, verification suggestion) and a runnable file rather than just a derivation. Proportional payoff.
